content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF NIELSEN, MERKSAMER, HODGSON, PARRINELLO & MUELLER
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

MARIN COUNTY
591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000
MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941
TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800

SAN FRANCISCO
650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
TELEPHONE (415) 989-6800

770 L STREET, SUITE 800
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752

March 29, 1990

Ms. Kathryn Donovan
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, 8th Floor
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Request For Advice

Dear Ms. Donovan:

This is to follow up on a telephone inquiry concerning the application of Government Code section 84302.5 and FPPC Regulation 18432.5 regarding who is an "intermediary for the delivery of a contribution."

1. FACTS

The facts in this situation are as follows: Mr. A is one of five co-proponents (Elections Code § 3502; Govt. Code § 82047.6) of an initiative measure currently being circulated for qualification for the November 1990 statewide ballot. CAREFUL is a recipient committee primarily formed to support the qualification and passage of the ballot measure (Govt. Code §§ 82013(a) and 82047.5). Mr. A also serves as a member of the five person steering committee of CAREFUL. As such, he is a controlling proponent of the CAREFUL committee (Govt. Code § 82016). Mr. A also is an official of one of the organizations that are sponsoring the ballot measure. Mr. A intends to collect and transmit campaign contributions to CAREFUL's treasurer.

2. QUESTION

Is Mr. A an "intermediary for the delivery of a contribution" from contributors to his controlled ballot measure committee?

3. OUR ANALYSIS

Proposition 68:§ 84302.5, determined to be valid by the Commission (In re Bell (1988) 11 FPPC Ops. 1), requires disclosure of persons who are intermediaries for the delivery of campaign contributions.

Ms. Kathryn Donovan  
March 29, 1990  
Page 2

In Regulation 18432.5, adopted October 13, 1989, the Commission delineated who does and does not constitute an intermediary for the delivery of a contribution. While Regulation 18432.5 does not specifically exempt a ballot measure proponent who controls the committee to which the contribution is made, subdivision (c)(3) does exempt a candidate who controls the committee to which the contribution is made from being deemed as such an "intermediary."

Inasmuch as a controlling proponent of a ballot measure committee is in the same position relative to such a committee as a candidate is to a committee he or she controls, we believe it would be appropriate to consider the controlling ballot measure proponent as exempt from the requirement to be treated as such an intermediary. The same policy considerations that were applicable to the specific exemptions contained in the regulation apply in this situation.

Thank you for your consideration of this request for advice.

Sincerely,

CHARLES H. BELL, JR.

CHB:deh  
6472.01
